text: This is Dustin Baldwin here. We're going to be going through a robotic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon begins port placement for the robotic cholecystectomy, inserting a varus needle at Palmer's point in the left upper quadrant. The next port is placed just to the right of the umbilicus in the midline, followed by a right lateral port and a final port in the left upper quadrant, forming a hockey stick configuration.

With the ports in place, the surgeon docks the robotic system and adjusts the patient positioning to seventeen degrees of reverse Trendelenburg and seven degrees of right side up. The camera is inserted into port number two, the bipolar instrument into port number one, the hook into port number three, and the pro grasp into arm four. A suction irrigator is available for retraction and field clearing if needed.
The surgeon uses arm four of the robotic system to retract the gallbladder cephalad, initiating the elective cholecystectomy procedure.

Using robotic arms one and three equipped with bipolar and hook instruments, the surgeon frees omental attachments to obtain the critical view of safety. The camera provides a clear view of the cystic triangle, showing both anterior and posterior aspects of the gallbladder. The surgeon clears fatty tissue from the hepatic triangle to identify the cystic artery and duct, while ensuring the critical view of safety by removing fibro fatty tissue from the hepatocystic triangle and exposing two structures entering the gallbladder with the liver visible in the background. The posterior peritoneum on the gallbladder is opened to facilitate medial and lateral retraction.

The surgeon uses ICG imaging to identify the common bile duct and cystic duct as the dissection progresses. The liver is clearly visible in the background, with two distinct structures entering the gallbladder, achieving the critical view of safety. A small vein is cauterized, and the fibro fatty tissue is dissected, preparing for clipping.

The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to confirm the anatomical structures before clipping. The first clip is placed on the cystic artery, followed by clipping the cystic duct. Both the cystic artery and duct are double clipped. The hook electrocautery is then used to divide these structures.

The surgeon uses the coagulation function on the cystic artery and the cutting function on the cystic duct. With the gallbladder now freed, it is retracted and maneuvered using robotic arms one and four to detach it from the liver.

The assistant removes the hook and inserts a retrieval bag. The surgeon uses both hands to place the gallbladder into the bag and extracts it through port number three, concluding the cholecystectomy procedure.
